title: Ergodicity

content.statement: Time averages and ensemble averages are not the same, protect against ruin.
content.question: Am I taking any bet whose worst outcome would actually ruin me?
content.mental_model: Casino, the average gambler wins on paper, the average individual goes broke.
content.business_lens: Avoid bets where a single bad outcome wipes out the company, even if expected value is positive.
content.life_lens: Survival comes first; optimize for time-average growth, not ensemble expected value.
sop: Identify bets with non-zero ruin probability
Reject them regardless of expected value
Use fractional sizing for bets with edge
Diversify so no single outcome can ruin you
content.explainer: A coin-flip with double-or-zero has positive expected value but guarantees ruin if repeated. Ergodicity explains why diversification matters, why VC firms make many small bets, and why personal-finance survival comes before optimization. Most "rational" expected-value calculations ignore the path to ruin.
questions: Am I taking any bet whose worst outcome would actually ruin me?
If you took this exact bet 100 times in a row yourself, not 100 different people taking it once, would you still come out ahead, or would one bad run wipe you out?
What's the largest single bet in your business right now, and have you sized it using how much you could survive losing, or how much you hope to gain?

definitions.def: A way to average values that are multiplied together.

content.equation_example: Three years of returns: plus 50%, plus 50%, negative 70%. Arithmetic average equals plus 10% per year, which sounds fine. Geometric mean, the actual compounded result, works out to roughly negative 21% per year, a real decline the arithmetic mean hides. A higher geometric mean means your real-world compounded growth is healthy; a big gap between arithmetic and geometric mean is a warning that a single bad outcome is quietly destroying you over time.
content.closing_statement: Survive first; then let compounding do the rest.

caption:
Principle Nº 327: Ergodicity

Time averages and ensemble averages are not the same, protect against ruin. A coin-flip with double-or-zero has positive expected value but guarantees ruin if repeated.

Ask yourself: Am I taking any bet whose worst outcome would actually ruin me?
